title: Nested Struct Address Book
prompt_md:
## Goal
Model a person whose data includes a nested address, using one struct nested inside another.

Define `struct Address` with members `std::string city` and `int zip`, and `struct Person` with members `std::string name` and `Address address`.

## Input
Two people, each described by three lines:
- name (single word)
- city (single word)
- zip (integer)

So lines 1-3 describe person 1 and lines 4-6 describe person 2.

## Output
Three lines:
```
<name1>: <city1> <zip1>
<name2>: <city2> <zip2>
sizeof(Person) = <size>
```
Access the nested city/zip through the `Person` object (chained member selection). Print `sizeof(Person)` for the last line.

## Constraints
- Initialize each `Person` with aggregate initialization, including inner braces for the nested `Address`.

## Notes
- `sizeof(Person)` is platform-dependent; the judge runs 64-bit g++ on Linux. Trust the judge's expected output.

test_cases.label: Sample -- two people with different cities
Hidden -- same city for both people
Hidden -- short names and small zip codes

title: Sensor Report with Padding Discovery
prompt_md:
## Goal
Report a series of sensor readings using a struct, then reveal how struct padding makes the struct larger than the sum of its members.

Define `struct Reading` with members in exactly this order: `char grade`, `double value`, `int id`. This ordering deliberately introduces padding, because `double` needs 8-byte alignment but follows a 1-byte `char`.

## Input
- Line 1: an integer `N` (1 <= N <= 5)
- Next `N` groups of three lines: a grade character (`A`/`B`/`C`), a floating-point value, and an integer id.

## Output
- `N` lines, one per reading:
  ```
  Sensor <id>: <value> [Grade <grade>]
  ```
  with `<value>` shown to one decimal place.
- Then a blank line, then three lines:
  ```
  sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int) = <sum>
  sizeof(Reading) = <actual>
  Padding bytes = <actual - sum>
  ```

## Constraints
- Keep the member order `char`, `double`, `int`.
- Compute the three size values with `sizeof` expressions; do not hardcode the numbers.
- Use `std::fixed` and `std::setprecision(1)` for the reading values.

test_cases.label: Sample -- three readings with mixed grades
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sensor 101: 98.6 [Grade A]
Sensor 202: 72.0 [Grade B]
Sensor 303: 55.3 [Grade C]

sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int) = 13
sizeof(Reading) = 24
Padding bytes = 11

test_cases.label: Hidden -- single reading
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sensor 1: 100.0 [Grade A]

sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int) = 13
sizeof(Reading) = 24
Padding bytes = 11

test_cases.label: Hidden -- five readings all grade B
test_cases.expected_stdout:
Sensor 1: 10.5 [Grade B]
Sensor 2: 20.3 [Grade B]
Sensor 3: 30.7 [Grade B]
Sensor 4: 40.1 [Grade B]
Sensor 5: 50.9 [Grade B]

sizeof(char) + sizeof(double) + sizeof(int) = 13
sizeof(Reading) = 24
Padding bytes = 11
